_meta.pass: Pass 1.9 — corpus density expansion (audit fix for connected-pool dilution)
_meta.validation_status: DRAFT — pending native-speaker validation by Leandro
_meta.notes: v1.5 expanded the ES misuse corpus from 35 to 120 misuses, ensuring every false friend has at least 3 misuse sentences. This addresses the audit finding on 'connected pool dilution': Level 2 can now reliably draw all of its trap tiles from the connected pool (sentences based on Level 1's traps) without falling back to top-up from the broader pool. Sentence IDs were renumbered sequentially within each based_on group to eliminate duplicates.

level1_words.notes: Classic false friend. EN means 'real'; ES means 'current'. The trap fires in both directions.
The most famous EN-ES false friend. Note: 'embarazada' is the feminine form; the masculine 'embarazado' exists but is rarely used in the same sense.
EN 'sensible' = ES 'sensato' / 'razonable'. ES 'sensible' = EN 'sensitive'.
Medical false friend. ES 'constipado' = EN 'has a cold'. EN 'constipated' = ES 'estreñido'.
ES 'éxito' = EN 'success'. EN 'exit' = ES 'salida'.
ES 'librería' = EN 'bookshop'. EN 'library' = ES 'biblioteca'.
ES 'carpeta' = EN 'folder'. EN 'carpet' = ES 'alfombra'.
ES 'fábrica' = EN 'factory'. EN 'fabric' = ES 'tela'.
ES 'ropa' = EN 'clothes'. EN 'rope' = ES 'cuerda'.
Significant register difference. ES 'molestar' is neutral and very common; EN 'molest' has shifted to a strongly negative meaning. A speaker confusing these can cause real distress.
ES 'asistir' primarily means 'to attend' (e.g., asistir a una reunión = attend a meeting). It can also mean 'help' in some contexts but the primary meaning is 'attend'. EN 'assist' = ES 'ayudar'.
ES 'introducir' = EN 'insert'. EN 'introduce' (as in 'introduce a person') = ES 'presentar'.

meanings.en: to be present at, to go to
meanings.es: to serve, to take care of, to attend to (e.g., a customer)

level1_words.notes: Tricky pair because both touch on the idea of 'attending'. EN 'attend a meeting' = ES 'asistir a una reunión'. ES 'atender un cliente' = EN 'serve a customer'.
Partial false friend. ES 'discusión' can mean a calm discussion in formal contexts but commonly implies an argument or disagreement. EN 'discussion' = ES 'conversación' or 'debate'.

meanings.en: in the end, finally, after some time

level1_words.notes: ES 'eventualmente' = EN 'possibly' or 'occasionally'. EN 'eventually' = ES 'finalmente' or 'al final'. Common ESL/SSL error.

meanings.en: compassionate, showing concern for others' feelings

level1_words.notes: ES 'simpático' = EN 'nice' or 'likeable'. EN 'sympathetic' = ES 'compasivo' or 'comprensivo'.

meanings.en: substance added to food to prevent spoilage

level1_words.notes: Memorably awkward false friend. EN 'preservative' = ES 'conservante'. ES 'preservativo' = EN 'condom'.
ES 'largo' = EN 'long'. EN 'large' = ES 'grande'.

meanings.en: a person's profession or occupation over time

level1_words.notes: ES 'carrera' most often means a degree program (estudiar una carrera). EN 'career' = ES 'trayectoria profesional' or 'profesión'.
ES 'decepción' = EN 'disappointment'. EN 'deception' = ES 'engaño'.

meanings.en: a settlement reached by mutual concession

level1_words.notes: ES 'compromiso' = EN 'commitment' (e.g., compromiso matrimonial = engagement). EN 'compromise' = ES 'acuerdo' or 'concesión mutua'.

meanings.es: to endure, tolerate, put up with

level1_words.notes: ES 'soportar' = EN 'endure' or 'tolerate'. EN 'support' = ES 'apoyar' (emotionally) or 'sostener' (physically).

meanings.en: to become aware of, to understand suddenly
meanings.es: to carry out, to perform, to accomplish

level1_words.notes: ES 'realizar' = EN 'perform' or 'carry out'. EN 'realize' = ES 'darse cuenta de'. FLAGGED: EN influence has caused some drift in casual ES usage; validate against your dialect.
ES 'asignatura' = EN 'subject' or 'course'. EN 'signature' = ES 'firma'. The words don't share roots but look similar enough to confuse learners.

meanings.en: to include as a necessary part

level1_words.notes: ES 'envolver' = EN 'wrap' (e.g., envolver un regalo). EN 'involve' = ES 'implicar' or 'involucrar'.
ES 'jubilación' = EN 'retirement'. EN 'jubilation' = ES 'júbilo'. The roots are connected but meanings have diverged.

meanings.en: an educational talk given to an audience

level1_words.notes: ES 'lectura' = EN 'reading'. EN 'lecture' = ES 'conferencia' or 'clase'.

meanings.en: a visible trace, sign, or symbol

level1_words.notes: ES 'marca' primarily means 'brand' (Coca-Cola es una marca). EN 'mark' = ES 'señal' or 'huella'. Partial false friend — 'marca' can mean 'mark' in some contexts, but the brand meaning dominates.

meanings.en: to feign, to act as if something is true when it isn't
meanings.es: to intend, to aspire, to claim (a position or person)

level1_words.notes: ES 'pretender' = EN 'intend' or 'aspire to'. EN 'pretend' = ES 'fingir' or 'aparentar'.
Partial false friend. ES 'destino' most commonly means 'destination' (¿Cuál es tu destino?). EN 'destiny' = ES 'destino' only in poetic/literary use; everyday meaning differs.

meanings.en: a place where people live temporarily in tents
meanings.es: field, countryside; also 'field' in academic sense

level1_words.notes: ES 'campo' = EN 'field' or 'countryside' (campo de fútbol = soccer field, vivir en el campo = live in the country). EN 'camp' = ES 'campamento'.

meanings.en: a reason given in support of a point; also a heated dispute
meanings.es: plot of a story or film; also 'reasoning' in logical context

level1_words.notes: ES 'argumento' primarily means 'plot' (el argumento de la película). EN 'argument' (dispute) = ES 'discusión' or 'pelea'. Partial overlap in formal logic contexts.

meanings.en: a written or printed announcement; also attention paid to something

level1_words.notes: ES 'noticia' = EN 'news'. EN 'notice' (announcement) = ES 'aviso' or 'anuncio'.
ES 'casual' = EN 'accidental' or 'by chance' (un encuentro casual = a chance encounter). EN 'casual' (informal) = ES 'informal' or 'desenfadado'.

meanings.en: to deliberately pay no attention to
meanings.es: to not know, to be unaware of

level1_words.notes: ES 'ignorar' primarily means 'to not know' (ignoro la respuesta = I don't know the answer). EN 'ignore' = ES 'hacer caso omiso' or 'desatender'. FLAGGED: partial overlap in modern usage but the false friend holds in standard register.

meanings.en: a public notice promoting a product or service

level1_words.notes: ES 'advertencia' = EN 'warning'. EN 'advertisement' = ES 'anuncio' or 'publicidad'.
ES 'delito' = EN 'crime'. EN 'delight' = ES 'placer' or 'deleite'. Memorable false friend because the meanings are so opposite in tone.
ES 'pariente' = EN 'relative' (any family member). EN 'parent' = ES 'padre' or 'madre'.
ES 'vaso' = EN 'glass' (drinking vessel). EN 'vase' = ES 'jarrón' or 'florero'.

meanings.en: a written message; also a single character of the alphabet
meanings.es: a single character of the alphabet; also 'lyrics' (of a song)

level1_words.notes: Partial false friend. Both share the alphabet-character meaning. ES 'letra' (the message) = EN 'carta'. ES 'letra' (song lyrics) = EN 'lyrics'. Trap fires when EN speaker uses 'letter' for 'message' and assumes ES 'letra' works the same way.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning. Note ES doesn't double the L (no 'colour' equivalent).
Same meaning.
Same meaning. (Note: ES 'radio' can also mean 'radius' in geometry, but the broadcast meaning is the relevant one.)
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning. Note ES uses '-dad' suffix where EN uses '-ty'.
Same meaning.
Same meaning, identical spelling.
Same meaning, identical spelling.
Same meaning. Note ES adds final -a (gendered noun).
Same meaning (the time unit, not the adjective).
Same meaning.
Same meaning.

meanings.en: open to or shared by all
meanings.es: open to or shared by all

level1_words.notes: Same meaning. Both languages also use it as a noun for 'audience'.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning, identical spelling.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.
Same meaning. Both can mean computer memory too.
Same meaning.
Same meaning.

level2_sentences.sentence: Nobody knows the actual cost of the project.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use of 'actual' meaning 'real'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I will email you the actual schedule, not the old one.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'current', which is the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse: the writer is treating EN 'actual' as if it meant ES 'actual' (current). The intended word would be 'current' or 'latest'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito el precio actual del producto.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use of 'actual' meaning 'current'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi hermana está embarazada de cinco meses.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: Estaba muy embarazada cuando me caí en público.
level2_sentences.notes: Classic misuse. The intended word is 'avergonzada'.

level2_sentences.sentence: She made a sensible decision to leave early.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use.

level2_sentences.sentence: He's very sensible — he cries at every movie.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'sensitive', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'sensitive' = ES 'sensible'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Es una persona muy sensible al ruido.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: No iré a trabajar, estoy muy constipado.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use. The speaker has a cold.

level2_sentences.sentence: I'm constipated — I think I caught it from my coworker.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means they have a cold (Spanish meaning)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'constipated' refers to bowel issues; the speaker is treating it as ES 'constipado'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La película fue un gran éxito en taquilla.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: Por favor, use el éxito de emergencia más cercano.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'exit', confusing it with EN
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'salida'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré tres libros nuevos en la librería del centro.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use. The speaker bought books at a bookshop.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a la librería a estudiar y devolver libros.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'library', confusing with EN
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'biblioteca' (you don't return books to a bookshop).

level2_sentences.sentence: Guardé los documentos en una carpeta azul.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: We need to clean the carpet before the documents arrive.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use, included as a contrasting safe tile.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi padre trabaja en una fábrica de coches.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: This dress is made of expensive Italian fabric.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use, contrasting safe tile.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito lavar la ropa antes del viaje.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré ropa nueva para escalar la montaña.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer probably means 'rope', confusing with EN
level2_sentences.notes: FLAGGED FOR REVIEW: This trap is soft. The sentence is grammatically valid Spanish meaning 'I bought new clothes for climbing.' May need replacement with a clearer trap.

level2_sentences.sentence: No quiero molestar, pero tengo una pregunta.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use, common polite phrase.

level2_sentences.sentence: Sorry to molest you, but do you have a moment?
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'bother', not the modern EN sense
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse with strong consequences. The intended word is 'bother' or 'disturb'. This is a trap that real Spanish speakers fall into in English.

level2_sentences.sentence: Pienso asistir a la conferencia el viernes.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: Can you assist the meeting tomorrow morning?
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'attend', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'attend'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Introduce la moneda en la ranura.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use. (Note: imperative 'introduce', not infinitive 'introducir'.)

level2_sentences.sentence: Please introduce your card into the machine.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'insert', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'insert'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuvimos una discusión muy fuerte anoche.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use, implying argument.

level2_sentences.sentence: The team had a productive discussion about the budget.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use, contrasting safe tile.

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventualmente trabajo los sábados, pero no siempre.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'occasionally'. FLAGGED: confirm this reads as natural Spanish.

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventually I help in the office on weekends.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'occasionally', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'eventually' = 'in the end', not 'occasionally'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tu primo es muy simpático, me cae genial.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use. FLAGGED: 'me cae genial' may be too colloquial; alternatives possible.

level2_sentences.sentence: Your cousin is so sympathetic — everyone enjoys his company.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'nice', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'nice' or 'friendly'.

level2_sentences.sentence: This bread has no artificial preservatives.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use.

level2_sentences.sentence: Check the label for preservatives before eating it.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use, additional contrast tile. (Deliberately did not write a misuse sentence using 'preservativo' to mean 'condom' because it would be off-tone for the brief.)

level2_sentences.sentence: El río más largo de Europa es el Volga.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use.

level2_sentences.sentence: I need a large box to ship this manuscript.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use, contrasting safe tile.

level2_sentences.sentence: Estudio la carrera de medicina en la universidad.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'degree program'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I want to study a career in engineering at MIT.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'degree program', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. 'Study a career' is not idiomatic English. Intended word is 'degree' or 'major'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Su renuncia fue una gran decepción para el equipo.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'disappointment'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Her resignation was a deception for the whole team.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'disappointment', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'disappointment'. EN 'deception' implies trickery, which would change the meaning entirely.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tienen un compromiso muy serio desde hace años.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'serious relationship' or 'engagement'.

level2_sentences.sentence: We've been in a serious compromise for several years.
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. 'In a compromise' suggests negotiation; the intended meaning is 'in a committed relationship'.

level2_sentences.sentence: No puedo soportar a mi vecino, hace mucho ruido.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: to tolerate, to put up with
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'tolerate'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I can't support my neighbor — he's too noisy.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'tolerate', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'tolerate' or 'stand'. 'Can't support' suggests not endorsing them, which doesn't fit context.

level2_sentences.sentence: El director va a realizar una nueva película el próximo año.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'to make/produce'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I didn't realize the meeting until I read your email.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use meaning 'become aware'. Contrasting safe tile.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi asignatura favorita siempre fue la historia.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'school subject'.

level2_sentences.sentence: My favorite signature in school was always history.
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'subject'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a envolver el regalo con papel dorado.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'to wrap'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I'll involve the gift with golden paper.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'wrap', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'wrap'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi padre disfruta de su jubilación viajando por Europa.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'retirement'.

level2_sentences.sentence: My father enjoys his jubilation traveling across Europe.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'retirement', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'retirement'. 'Enjoys his jubilation' is grammatically off and meaningfully wrong.

level2_sentences.sentence: La lectura de novelas es mi pasatiempo favorito.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'reading'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Reading lectures of novels is my favorite hobby.
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. 'Reading lectures of novels' is awkward; the writer is treating 'lecture' as if it meant 'reading' (the ES meaning).

level2_sentences.sentence: Esta marca de café es la más popular del país.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'brand'.

level2_sentences.sentence: This mark of coffee is the most popular in the country.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'brand', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. 'Mark of coffee' is not idiomatic; the intended word is 'brand'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ella pretende ser elegida presidenta de la asociación.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'intends'.

level2_sentences.sentence: She pretends to be elected president of the association.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'intends to', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'pretends to be elected' implies she's faking the election, which is the opposite of the intended meaning.

level2_sentences.sentence: Nuestro destino final es la ciudad de Roma.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'destination'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Our final destiny is the city of Rome.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'destination', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'destiny' implies fate; the intended word is 'destination'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mis abuelos viven en el campo, lejos de la ciudad.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'countryside'.

level2_sentences.sentence: My grandparents live in the camp, far from the city.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'countryside', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. 'Live in the camp' suggests a temporary settlement; the intended word is 'countryside'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El argumento de la película es muy original.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'plot'.

level2_sentences.sentence: The argument of the film is very original.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'plot', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'argument of the film' is awkward; the intended word is 'plot' or 'story'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tengo una buena noticia que compartir contigo.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'news'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I have a good notice to share with you.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'news', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'a good notice' suggests a written announcement; the intended word is 'news'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Fue un encuentro casual en el aeropuerto.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'by chance'.

level2_sentences.sentence: It was a casual meeting at the airport.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use meaning 'informal'. Contrasting safe tile demonstrating both meanings.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ignoro completamente cuál es la respuesta correcta.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'I don't know'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I completely ignore which answer is correct.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'don't know', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'ignore' means deliberately disregard; the intended meaning is 'don't know'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El paquete trae una advertencia sobre el uso de niños.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'warning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: The package has an advertisement about children's use.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'warning', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'advertisement' is a marketing notice; the intended word is 'warning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El robo es un delito grave en este país.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'crime'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Theft is a serious delight in this country.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'crime', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse with comically opposite meaning. The intended word is 'crime'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tengo muchos parientes lejanos en Argentina.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'relatives'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I have many distant parents in Argentina.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'relatives', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'parents' refers only to mother and father; 'distant parents' is nonsensical.

level2_sentences.sentence: Por favor, tráeme un vaso de agua fría.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'glass'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Please bring me a vase of cold water.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'glass', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'vase' holds flowers, not water for drinking. Intended word is 'glass'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La letra de esta canción es muy poética.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'lyrics'.

level2_sentences.sentence: The letter of this song is very poetic.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'lyrics', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'letter of a song' is not idiomatic; the intended word is 'lyrics'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El mesero va a atender nuestra mesa pronto.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: to serve, to take care of
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'to serve' (a customer).

level2_sentences.sentence: The waiter will attend our table soon.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'serve', the Spanish meaning
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. EN 'attend a table' is not idiomatic; the intended word is 'serve' or 'wait on'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La situación actual del país es complicada.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'current'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I'm so embarazada I can barely speak right now.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'embarrassed', mixing in the Spanish word
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse via code-switching. The speaker has reached for 'embarazada' thinking it means 'embarrassed'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tomó una decisión sensible para evitar problemas.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'sensato' (reasonable), confusing with EN 'sensible'
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. ES 'decisión sensible' would mean 'sensitive decision'; the intended meaning is 'wise/reasonable decision' = 'decisión sensata'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tengo que comprar pañuelos, estoy constipado.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use. Tissues confirm the cold context.

level2_sentences.sentence: His debut album was an instant exit on the charts.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'success' (Spanish meaning of éxito)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. The intended word is 'success'.

level2_sentences.sentence: I bought this novel at a bookstore called The Library.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the bookstore is named 'The Library' but is actually a bookstore
level2_sentences.notes: Correct EN use of 'library' as a proper name. Pedagogically interesting because both meanings are present.

level2_sentences.sentence: Please put these files in the blue carpet on my desk.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'folder' (Spanish meaning of carpeta)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. Files don't go on a 'blue carpet'; the intended word is 'folder'.

level2_sentences.sentence: He works at a large fabric that produces cars.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'factory' (Spanish meaning of fábrica)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. A 'fabric' doesn't produce cars; the intended word is 'factory'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Me probé la ropa antes de comprarla.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'clothes'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Los mosquitos me molestan mucho en verano.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'bother'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mañana voy a asistir a la boda de mi prima.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'attend'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Hay que introducir el código en el cajero.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'insert/enter'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La discusión entre ellos terminó muy mal.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use; the bad ending confirms the argument meaning.

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventualmente trabajamos los fines de semana, no es habitual.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'occasionally'. Context confirms by saying 'not habitual'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El nuevo profesor es muy simpático con los estudiantes.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'nice'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Este pan no contiene preservativos artificiales.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'food preservatives', confusing with EN
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. ES 'preservativos' = condoms; the intended word is 'conservantes'.

level2_sentences.sentence: She has very large hair that reaches her waist.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'long' (Spanish meaning of largo)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. Hair length is described with 'long', not 'large'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi hermano corrió la carrera de cinco kilómetros.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'race'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Me llevé una gran decepción con el resultado del examen.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'disappointment'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tengo un compromiso de trabajo el viernes por la noche.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'work commitment'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Estos zapatos no soportan la lluvia muy bien.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'withstand'.

level2_sentences.sentence: The company will realize a major project next month.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the writer means 'carry out' (Spanish meaning of realizar)
level2_sentences.notes: Misuse. Companies don't 'realize a project' (in the awareness sense); the intended word is 'carry out' or 'complete'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Esta asignatura es obligatoria para todos los estudiantes.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'subject'.

level2_sentences.sentence: ¿Me puedes envolver el libro para regalo?
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'wrap'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Después de cuarenta años de trabajo, espera su jubilación.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'retirement'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La lectura del informe nos llevó toda la mañana.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'reading'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré zapatos de una marca italiana.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'brand'.

level2_sentences.sentence: No pretendo ofender a nadie con mis comentarios.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'intend'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El destino del vuelo es Madrid.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'destination'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Trabaja en el campo de la inteligencia artificial.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'field' (area of expertise).

level2_sentences.sentence: El argumento de la novela es muy complejo.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'plot'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La noticia del accidente nos sorprendió a todos.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'news'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Nos conocimos de manera casual en el parque.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'by chance'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ignoramos cuándo llegará el paquete.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'we don't know'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El médico me dio una advertencia sobre los efectos secundarios.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'warning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Conducir sin licencia es un delito en este estado.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'crime'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi tío es el único pariente que me queda en España.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'relative'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El vaso se rompió cuando se cayó al suelo.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'glass'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La letra de tu nombre comienza con M.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use meaning 'alphabetical letter'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El precio actual del libro es veinte dólares.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse via English influence. The speaker means 'real/actual' price as in EN, but ES 'actual' = 'current'. Should be 'real' or 'verdadero'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Estoy muy embarazada por lo que dije en la reunión.
level2_sentences.notes: Classic Spanglish error. Speaker means 'embarrassed' (avergonzada), not 'pregnant' (the actual ES meaning).

level2_sentences.sentence: Tomé el éxito de emergencia para salir del edificio.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'exit' (salida), not 'success' (the actual ES meaning of éxito).

level2_sentences.sentence: El restaurante tiene una carpeta roja muy elegante.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'carpet' (alfombra), not 'folder' (the actual ES meaning of carpeta).

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré tres metros de fábrica para hacer un vestido.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'fabric/cloth' (tela), not 'factory' (the actual ES meaning of fábrica).

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a la librería a estudiar para el examen final.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'library' (biblioteca), not 'bookstore' (the actual ES meaning of librería).

level2_sentences.sentence: El profesor fue acusado de molestar a una estudiante.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'molest' (abusar), but ES 'molestar' = 'bother/annoy'. Subtle but pedagogically important — the false friend can cause real misunderstanding.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito asistir al niño con su tarea de matemáticas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'help' (ayudar), not 'attend' (the actual ES meaning of asistir).

level2_sentences.sentence: Permíteme introducirte a mi amiga Carla.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to introduce a person' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'introduce a person' (presentar), not 'insert/introduce an object' (the actual ES meaning).

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuvimos una discusión muy agradable sobre películas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. ES 'discusión' implies argument/disagreement, so 'discusión agradable' is contradictory. Speaker means 'conversación'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventualmente terminaré mi tesis, no sé cuándo.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'eventually/at some point' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'eventually' (algún día / con el tiempo), but ES 'eventualmente' = 'occasionally'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Lamento tu pérdida, te tengo mucho simpático.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'sympathy' (compasión / pésame), but ES 'simpático' = 'nice/friendly'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito una ropa para amarrar este paquete.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'rope' (cuerda / soga), but ES 'ropa' = 'clothing'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El mago realizó una gran decepción frente al público.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'deception' (engaño), but ES 'decepción' = 'disappointment'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Después de mucha discusión, llegamos a un compromiso.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'compromise' (acuerdo / concesión mutua), but ES 'compromiso' = 'commitment/engagement'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mis padres siempre me soportan en mis decisiones.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'support' (apoyan), but ES 'soportar' = 'tolerate/endure'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ahora realizo que cometí un error grave en el informe.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse, increasingly common under English influence. Speaker means EN 'realize' (darme cuenta de), but ES 'realizar' = 'carry out/perform'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito tu asignatura en este formulario, por favor.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'signature' (firma), but ES 'asignatura' = 'school subject'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El proyecto va a envolver a todo el equipo de marketing.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'involve' (involucrar), but ES 'envolver' = 'wrap'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Asistí a una lectura sobre física cuántica en la universidad.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'lecture' (conferencia / clase magistral), but ES 'lectura' = 'reading'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Hay una marca en la pared que parece sangre.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'mark/stain' (mancha), but ES 'marca' typically means 'brand'. Partial false friend — context helps clarify.

level2_sentences.sentence: El niño pretende estar dormido cuando llamo a la puerta.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'pretend' (finge / aparenta), but ES 'pretender' = 'intend/aspire'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Creo en el destino, todo lo que pasa estaba escrito.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use in the literary 'fate' sense. Both meanings of destino exist; this one is valid.

level2_sentences.sentence: Vamos al campo de verano de los niños este fin de semana.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'summer camp' (campamento), but ES 'campo' = 'field/countryside'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuvimos un argumento muy fuerte sobre la política familiar.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'argument/dispute' (discusión / pelea), but ES 'argumento' = 'plot of a story' or formal 'reasoning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Pegaron una noticia en la pared sobre la fiesta del sábado.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'notice/posted announcement' (aviso / anuncio), but ES 'noticia' = 'news'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy con ropa casual a la oficina los viernes.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse, increasingly common. Speaker means EN 'casual/informal' (informal / desenfadada), but standard ES 'casual' = 'by chance/accidental'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Vi una advertencia muy creativa de coca-cola en la televisión.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'advertisement' (anuncio / publicidad), but ES 'advertencia' = 'warning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a la reunión con mis dos parientes esta noche.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'parents' (padres), but ES 'pariente' = 'any relative'. The 'two' makes the misuse plausible only as 'parents'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a ignorar tu mensaje a propósito porque me molestaste.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'deliberately ignore' (hacer caso omiso / no hacer caso), but standard ES 'ignorar' = 'not know'. Increasingly accepted in casual modern Spanish under English influence.

level2_sentences.sentence: Llevo tres días constipado y no puedo ir al baño.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'constipated/digestive issue' (estreñido), but ES 'constipado' = 'congested with a cold'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El médico me dijo que estoy constipado y debo comer más fibra.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The fibra cue makes the digestive meaning unmistakable. Speaker means 'estreñido', but said 'constipado'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi abuela está constipada porque no toma suficiente agua.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The water-intake context implicates digestive constipation, not a head cold.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a atender la conferencia de ciencia el sábado por la tarde.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to attend an event' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'attend' (asistir a), but ES 'atender' = 'to serve/take care of'. Common Spanglish error.

level2_sentences.sentence: No pude atender la boda de mi prima por el viaje de trabajo.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to attend an event' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'asistir a' (attend), not 'atender' (serve/take care of).

level2_sentences.sentence: Tengo que atender una reunión importante a las diez de la mañana.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to attend a meeting' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'attend a meeting' = 'asistir a una reunión'. The misuse is increasingly common in business Spanish under English influence.

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré un café largo en Starbucks porque tenía mucha sed.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'large' (grande), but ES 'largo' = 'long'. Very common in coffee/restaurant ordering Spanglish.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi camiseta talla larga ya no me queda bien después del verano.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Clothing-size context. EN 'large' = 'grande' in Spanish; 'largo/larga' = 'long'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito una caja larga para guardar todos estos libros.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Storage context implicates 'grande' (large), not 'larga' (long).

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi carrera en marketing va muy bien, ya soy gerente regional.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'professional career path' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'career' (trayectoria profesional), but ES 'carrera' = 'university degree' or 'race'. Subtle: 'carrera' can mean career in some contexts but not in this professional progression sense.

level2_sentences.sentence: Después de veinte años de carrera, finalmente me ascendieron a director.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Same drift — 'twenty years of career' implies the EN 'career' meaning, not ES 'university degree'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Cambié de carrera porque ya no me gustaba trabajar en finanzas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'change careers' = 'cambiar de profesión / trayectoria'. Speaker uses 'carrera' under English influence.

level2_sentences.sentence: Sentí mucha jubilación cuando supe que aprobé el examen final.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'jubilation' (júbilo / alegría), but ES 'jubilación' = 'retirement from work'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Hubo gran jubilación en la calle cuando ganó la selección argentina.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Sports celebration context implicates 'júbilo' (jubilation), but speaker used 'jubilación' (retirement).

level2_sentences.sentence: La jubilación de los fans en el concierto fue increíble.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'jubilation of fans' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Concert context. EN 'jubilation' = 'júbilo' or 'euforia', not ES 'jubilación' (retirement).

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi destino para las vacaciones de verano es la ciudad de Lyon.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use — 'destino' = 'travel destination'. This is a valid use; included to balance the corpus on this entry.

level2_sentences.sentence: Comer chocolate antes de dormir es mi pequeño delito de placer.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'guilty pleasure' or 'small sin' (pecado / placer culpable), but ES 'delito' = 'criminal offense'. The casual context makes the criminal meaning absurd.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi único delito fue comer la última galleta sin avisarle.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Calling eating a cookie a 'delito' (crime) is hyperbolic in EN but inappropriate in ES — speaker means 'pecado' or 'falta'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ese postre es un delito, está demasiado delicioso para ser legal.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'a sin/something forbidden' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Idiomatic English 'that dessert is a crime' (so good it should be illegal). The construction works in EN slang but not in standard ES.

level2_sentences.sentence: Decoramos la mesa con un vaso de flores rosas y blancas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'vase' = ES 'jarrón' or 'florero'; ES 'vaso' = 'drinking glass'. The flowers context makes the intended meaning clear.

level2_sentences.sentence: Compré un vaso antiguo de cerámica en el mercado de pulgas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Antique-store context implicates 'jarrón' (decorative vase), not 'vaso' (drinking glass).

level2_sentences.sentence: Cuidado con ese vaso de cristal, es muy frágil y costó mucho dinero.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. 'Cristal' (crystal) and 'frágil' (fragile) plus high cost suggest decorative item, not drinking glass.

level2_sentences.sentence: Te envío una letra para que la firmes y me la devuelvas pronto.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'letter (correspondence)' = ES 'carta'; ES 'letra' = 'single character/alphabet letter' or 'song lyrics'. Common Spanglish error.

level2_sentences.sentence: Recibí una letra del banco diciendo que mi cuenta tiene problemas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Bank correspondence context — EN 'letter' = 'carta', not 'letra'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi abuela siempre me escribe letras a mano con tinta azul.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'cartas' (letters/correspondence), but used 'letras' (alphabet characters) under English influence.

level2_sentences.sentence: El problema actual con el carro es que no arranca por las mañanas.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'the actual problem' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'actual' = 'real / verdadero' in Spanish; ES 'actual' = 'current'. The context of describing a specific problem implies the EN sense.

level2_sentences.sentence: Lo actual es que no tenemos dinero para pagar el alquiler este mes.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'the actual/real fact' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means EN 'the actual fact' (la verdad / lo real / lo cierto), not ES 'lo actual' (the current/present).

level2_sentences.sentence: Necesito tomar una decisión sensible sobre dónde invertir mis ahorros.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Classic false friend. EN 'sensible' (rational) = ES 'sensato / razonable'; ES 'sensible' = 'sensitive/emotionally aware'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi hermano siempre da consejos muy sensibles porque piensa antes de hablar.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Wise/thoughtful advice = 'sensato', not 'sensible' (which means emotionally sensitive in Spanish).

level2_sentences.sentence: La carpeta del salón es de lana y muy difícil de limpiar.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Wool material + cleaning context = 'alfombra' (carpet/rug). 'Carpeta' in ES = file folder, not floor covering.

level2_sentences.sentence: Pisé la carpeta con los zapatos sucios y mi madre se enojó.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'walked on the carpet' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Stepping on something = 'alfombra'. You don't step on a folder.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi madre cose vestidos con fábrica de algodón orgánico.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Sewing context = 'tela' (fabric). 'Fábrica' = factory.

level2_sentences.sentence: Esa fábrica de seda es demasiado fina para hacer cortinas.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Silk/curtains context = 'tela', not 'fábrica' (factory).

level2_sentences.sentence: Las autoridades investigan el caso del entrenador acusado de molestar menores.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse with serious implications. Speaker means EN 'molest/abuse' (abusar / acosar sexualmente), not ES 'molestar' (bother). The misuse can cause real misunderstanding in news/legal contexts.

level2_sentences.sentence: El sospechoso fue arrestado por intentar molestar a una niña en el parque.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Crime/police context. EN 'molest' (a child) = ES 'abusar de'. ES 'molestar' (bother) is far less serious.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi nuevo trabajo es asistir al gerente con tareas administrativas diarias.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to assist the manager' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'assist a manager' = 'ayudar al gerente' or 'ser asistente de'. ES 'asistir' = 'attend (an event)'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Voy a contratar a alguien para asistirme con la mudanza el sábado.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'to help me with the move' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'assist with the move' = 'ayudar con la mudanza'. ES 'asistir' is to attend.

level2_sentences.sentence: Quisiera introducirles al nuevo director del departamento de marketing.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'introduce someone' = 'presentar'; ES 'introducir' = insert (an object). Very common business Spanglish.

level2_sentences.sentence: Te quiero introducir a mi novio antes de que se vaya de viaje.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'introduce my boyfriend' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Speaker means 'presentar' (introduce a person). 'Introducir' implies physical insertion, awkward in social context.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuvimos una discusión productiva en la junta sobre el nuevo presupuesto.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. ES 'discusión' implies argument/disagreement. EN 'productive discussion' = 'conversación productiva' or 'debate productivo'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Después de una larga discusión amistosa, decidimos qué película ver.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. 'Discusión amistosa' is contradictory in standard ES (discusiones aren't friendly). Speaker means 'conversación' or 'charla'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventualmente voy a terminar mi novela, pero no tengo prisa.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'eventually' (algún día / al final / con el tiempo) ≠ ES 'eventualmente' (occasionally / from time to time).

level2_sentences.sentence: Eventualmente todos vamos a aprender a manejar las nuevas tecnologías.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'eventually all of us' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The 'todos' implies a future inevitability ('eventually everyone will'), the EN sense, not the ES 'occasionally'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Me siento muy simpático con su situación, debe ser difícil.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'sympathetic' (toward someone's pain) = 'comprensivo' or 'compasivo'. ES 'simpático' = 'nice/likeable'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El doctor fue muy simpático con mi diagnóstico, eso me ayudó mucho.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Medical context where 'simpático' would just mean the doctor was friendly, but speaker means EN 'sympathetic to my situation' (comprensivo).

level2_sentences.sentence: Esta mermelada no tiene preservativos, es completamente natural.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse with potentially embarrassing implications. EN 'preservatives' (in food) = ES 'conservantes'; ES 'preservativos' = 'condoms'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Lee la etiqueta para ver qué preservativos contiene esta marca.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The label-reading + brand context implicates food additives ('conservantes'), making the speaker's intended meaning clear despite the wrong word.

level2_sentences.sentence: El estafador realizó una decepción muy elaborada para robar el dinero.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Crime context. EN 'deception' = 'engaño / estafa'; ES 'decepción' = 'disappointment'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Cuidado con esa decepción, parece real pero es completamente falso.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The 'parece real pero es falso' framing makes the EN 'deception' meaning unmistakable.

level2_sentences.sentence: Después de horas de negociación llegamos a un compromiso aceptable para ambos.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Negotiation context. EN 'compromise' (middle-ground) = ES 'concesión mutua / acuerdo'; ES 'compromiso' = 'commitment / engagement'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuve que hacer un compromiso entre lo que quería y lo que podía pagar.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'made a compromise/concession' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'made a compromise' = 'cedí / hice una concesión'. ES 'compromiso' implies commitment, not the act of giving ground.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi mejor amiga me soporta emocionalmente cuando tengo problemas.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'supports me emotionally' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'supports emotionally' = 'apoya'; ES 'soportar' = 'tolerate / put up with'. The emocionalmente cue makes the misuse striking.

level2_sentences.sentence: Los empleados soportan al nuevo CEO porque tiene buenas ideas.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'support the CEO' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Workplace endorsement context. EN 'support the leader' = 'apoyan'. Standard ES 'soportar' here would mean 'tolerate/endure', which sounds critical.

level2_sentences.sentence: De repente realicé que había olvidado el pasaporte en casa.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Common modern Spanglish. EN 'realize' = 'darse cuenta'; ES 'realizar' = 'carry out / accomplish'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Cuando vi el video, realicé que mi hijo tenía mucho talento.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'realized my son was talented' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'realize about a person' = 'darme cuenta de'. Increasingly accepted in colloquial Spanish but flagged here as a misuse.

level2_sentences.sentence: Por favor, deja tu asignatura al final del documento legal.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Legal/document context. EN 'signature' = 'firma'; ES 'asignatura' = 'school subject'.

level2_sentences.sentence: La asignatura del artista famoso vale mucho dinero en subasta.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Auction/celebrity context. EN 'signature' (autograph) = 'firma' or 'autógrafo'; ES 'asignatura' is about school subjects.

level2_sentences.sentence: Esta nueva ley va a envolver muchos cambios para nuestra empresa.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Legal/business context. EN 'involve / entail' = 'implicar / suponer'; ES 'envolver' = 'wrap'.

level2_sentences.sentence: No quiero envolverme en los problemas familiares de mi vecino.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'get involved in problems' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'get involved in' = 'involucrarme' or 'meterme'. 'Envolverme' literally means to wrap myself, which doesn't make sense here.

level2_sentences.sentence: El profesor dio una lectura fascinante sobre la historia romana antigua.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'lecture' (academic talk) = 'conferencia / charla'; ES 'lectura' = 'reading' (the act of reading).

level2_sentences.sentence: Mañana hay una lectura pública en la universidad sobre cambio climático.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Public talk context. EN 'public lecture' = 'conferencia pública'; ES 'lectura' implies a reading session.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tiene una marca grande en la cara desde el accidente de bicicleta.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'a mark/scar on the face' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Body-injury context. EN 'mark / scar' = 'marca' is acceptable in some dialects, but 'cicatriz' is more precise. Speaker means physical mark/scar, which English speakers default to 'marca'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi camisa nueva tiene una marca de café que no se quita con nada.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Stain context. EN 'mark / stain' = ES 'mancha'; ES 'marca' is more precisely 'brand' or 'sign/imprint'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El gato pretende estar dormido pero solo está esperando que me vaya.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Common in heritage Spanish. EN 'pretend / feign' = 'fingir / aparentar'; ES 'pretender' = 'intend / aspire'.

level2_sentences.sentence: No pretendas que no sabes nada del asunto, todos vimos lo que pasó.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'don't pretend ignorance' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Confrontation context. EN 'pretend' = 'fingir'. Standard ES 'no pretendas' would mean 'don't aspire to', which makes no sense in confrontation.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi hijo va al campo de matemáticas durante todo el verano.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Camp context. EN 'math camp / summer camp' = 'campamento'; ES 'campo' = 'field / countryside'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El campo de música rock empezó la semana pasada en las montañas.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'rock music camp' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'music camp' = 'campamento de música'; ES 'campo' is field/countryside, not an organized camp/program.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi novio y yo tuvimos un argumento muy fuerte ayer por la noche.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'a fight/argument with partner' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'argument / fight' = 'discusión / pelea'; ES 'argumento' = 'plot of a story' or 'formal reasoning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Ese argumento entre los vecinos terminó cuando llamaron a la policía.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'argument/fight between neighbors' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Conflict requiring police involvement = 'pelea / disputa', not 'argumento' (which is a story plot).

level2_sentences.sentence: Pusieron una noticia en el tablero del edificio sobre la reunión de mañana.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'notice / posted announcement' = 'aviso / anuncio'; ES 'noticia' = 'news'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Recibí una noticia oficial del gobierno sobre los nuevos impuestos.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Government correspondence context. EN 'official notice' = 'aviso / notificación oficial'.

level2_sentences.sentence: El código de vestimenta de la empresa es casual los viernes.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'casual dress code' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Workplace dress-code context. EN 'casual dress' = 'informal'; standard ES 'casual' = 'by chance / accidental'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tuvimos una conversación casual sobre la película sin profundizar.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'casual conversation' = 'conversación informal / relajada'; ES 'casual' implies coincidental.

level2_sentences.sentence: La advertencia del nuevo coche eléctrico se está viendo en toda la ciudad.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Advertising context. EN 'advertisement' = 'anuncio / publicidad'; ES 'advertencia' = 'warning'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Esa advertencia de televisión es muy creativa y me hace reír siempre.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. TV/comedy context. 'Advertencia' (warning) doesn't make people laugh; speaker means 'anuncio' (commercial).

level2_sentences.sentence: Me sentí muy embarazada cuando me preguntó delante de todos.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Classic Spanglish error. EN 'embarrassed' = 'avergonzada / apenada'; ES 'embarazada' = 'pregnant'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Sigue las señales del éxito de emergencia para salir del edificio.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Building safety context. EN 'exit' = 'salida'; ES 'éxito' = 'success'. The signs context makes the misuse comical.

level2_sentences.sentence: La librería universitaria está abierta todos los días hasta las once.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'library' = 'biblioteca'; ES 'librería' = 'bookstore'. The university+late hours context implicates a study library.

level2_sentences.sentence: Tira la ropa por la ventana para escalar y bajar al primer piso.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Climbing/escape context. EN 'rope' = 'cuerda / soga'; ES 'ropa' = 'clothing'. Throwing clothing for climbing makes no sense.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi destino para el trabajo es siempre el mismo edificio en el centro.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use — 'destino' as a destination/place is valid. Marked as correct usage.

level2_sentences.sentence: Era mi destino encontrarte aquí, no podía haber sucedido de otra forma.
level2_sentences.notes: Correct ES use — 'destino' in the literary 'fate' sense. Both 'destination' and 'fate' meanings exist in Spanish; this is the latter.

level2_sentences.sentence: Decidí ignorar el correo electrónico porque no me parecía importante.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'deliberately ignore email' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'deliberately ignore' = 'hacer caso omiso / no hacer caso'; standard ES 'ignorar' = 'not know'. Increasingly accepted under English influence.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mi vecino me ignora cada vez que lo veo en el ascensor.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Standard ES 'ignorar' implies not knowing, but here speaker means active 'snubbing' = 'no me hace caso / me esquiva'.

level2_sentences.sentence: Llamé a mis parientes para decirles que llegaré tarde a la cena.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. The 'cena' (dinner) implies the speaker is calling immediate family ('padres'), not general relatives.

level2_sentences.sentence: Mis parientes son muy estrictos con las reglas de la casa.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. House rules implicate 'padres' (parents who set rules), not extended family ('parientes').

level2_sentences.sentence: Estoy convencido de que mi destino es ser un programador exitoso.
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'my destiny' (career calling) lands awkwardly; standard ES would say 'mi vocación' or 'mi camino'. 'Destino' is more about destination or external fate, not chosen calling.

level2_sentences.sentence: El destino de la empresa depende de las decisiones que tomemos hoy.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'fate/future of company' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. Business context. EN 'fate of the company' = ES 'futuro / suerte de la empresa'. 'Destino' here sounds overly literary/dramatic for corporate decision-making.

level2_sentences.sentence: Su destino estaba marcado desde el momento en que conoció a esa mujer.
level2_sentences.translation_meaning: the speaker means 'fate was sealed' (English-influenced misuse)
level2_sentences.notes: ES misuse. EN 'fate was sealed/marked' is idiomatic in English; ES would say 'su suerte estaba echada' or 'su camino estaba marcado'.
